topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The stage lights felt like physical blows, blinding and mocking. Elias stood backstage, his knuckles white where he gripped the worn leather of his guitar case. The sound bleeding through the thin wall—that melody, *his* melody, twisted and soured by another’s clumsy interpretation—hit him like a physical assault. He slammed the case shut, the resulting thud echoing the violent rhythm pounding in his chest. He stalked toward the door, his jaw tight enough to crack stone. When he finally saw the figures bathed in the harsh glare, a deep, guttural sound escaped his throat, a low vibration that seemed to shake the very floorboards. He didn't wait for an introduction; he just moved, a coiled spring about to snap, his eyes locked on the performer with a pure, burning resentment.

[story 2]

I watched from the shadows, my breath catching in ragged, shallow gasps. Every note they played was a deliberate, disrespectful mockery of the intricate tapestry I had woven in my head. It wasn't just wrong; it was an insult to the very soul of the composition. A hot flush crept up my neck, turning my skin prickly. When the final, discordant chord faded, I couldn't keep still. I took two swift steps forward, my boots scraping against the dusty floor, and I let out a sharp, ragged exhalation that was more of a hiss than a sound. My hands balled into fists so tight my nails bit into my palms. I needed to dismantle that performance, to shatter the illusion they were projecting.

[story 3]

The silence after the rendition was deafening, a vacuum sucking the air from the room. My vision tunneled, focusing solely on the deviation—the way they stretched the harmony, the careless timing. A tremor ran through my hands, making the silver rings on my fingers catch the light erratically. I felt a building pressure behind my sternum, a desperate need to intervene, to correct the grievous error in real-time. I pushed off the wall, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, crossing the space between the audience and the stage with furious speed. When I reached the edge of the performance area, I didn't speak; I simply stood there, radiating a palpable wave of sheer, unadulterated displeasure, my gaze a cold, unwavering indictment aimed directly at the musicians.
